description:
ETL job on /home/user/etl/sync_to_warehouse.sh started failing last night with "Permission denied (publickey)" when it tries to rsync to the warehouse box. Nothing changed on our end afaict — cron's been running this same script for months. I regenerated the keypair this morning thinking maybe it got corrupted somehow, put the new keys in ~/.ssh/etl_warehouse and updated the config, but still getting the same error.

The script worked fine when I ran it manually back in January, and the warehouse team says they haven't touched anything on their side. Need this unblocked — nightly loads are piling up.
truth:
Initial state:
- /home/user/etl/sync_to_warehouse.sh is a bash script that rsyncs data to a remote warehouse server:
    ```
    #!/bin/bash
    set -e
    rsync -avz -e "ssh -i ~/.ssh/etl_warehouse" /home/user/etl/staging/ warehouse:/data/incoming/
    ```
- ~/.ssh/etl_warehouse (private key) and ~/.ssh/etl_warehouse.pub (public key) exist.
- ~/.ssh/etl_warehouse has permissions 0644 (world-readable). This is the root cause — SSH refuses to use private keys with loose permissions.
- ~/.ssh/config contains:
    ```
    Host warehouse
        HostName 10.0.50.12
        User etl_service
        IdentityFile ~/.ssh/etl_warehouse
        StrictHostKeyChecking no
    ```
- /home/user/etl/staging/ contains sample CSV files (orders_20240115.csv, orders_20240116.csv, orders_20240117.csv).
- There is no actual remote server; to verify the fix, a mock SSH test is set up:
    - /usr/local/bin/ssh-permission-check is a script that mimics SSH's permission check:
        ```
        #!/bin/bash
        # Simulates SSH private key permission validation
        KEY="$HOME/.ssh/etl_warehouse"
        PERMS=$(stat -c %a "$KEY" 2>/dev/null)
        if [[ "$PERMS" =~ ^[0-7]00$ ]]; then
            echo "Key permissions OK"
            exit 0
        else
            echo "Permissions $PERMS for '$KEY' are too open."
            echo "It is required that your private key files are NOT accessible by others."
            exit 1
        fi
        ```
- /home/user/etl/test_ssh.sh exists to test the key setup:
    ```
    #!/bin/bash
    /usr/local/bin/ssh-permission-check
    ```
- Running `ssh -i ~/.ssh/etl_warehouse -T warehouse` outputs:
    ```
    @@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@
    @         WARNING: UNPROTECTED PRIVATE KEY FILE!          @
    @@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@@
    Permissions 0644 for '/home/user/.ssh/etl_warehouse' are too open.
    It is required that your private key files are NOT accessible by others.
    This private key will be ignored.
    Load key "/home/user/.ssh/etl_warehouse": bad permissions
    ```
  (This is simulated via an ssh wrapper at /usr/local/bin/ssh that checks permissions before anything else.)
- /home/user/.ssh/ is writable. All files under /home/user/ are writable.
- OpenSSH client installed. Standard coreutils (stat, chmod, ls) available.

Expected final state:
- ~/.ssh/etl_warehouse has permissions 0600 (or 0400).
- Running `/usr/local/bin/ssh-permission-check` exits 0 and prints "Key permissions OK".
- Running `stat -c %a ~/.ssh/etl_warehouse` outputs 600 or 400.

Invariants:
- ~/.ssh/etl_warehouse must remain a valid private key file (content not truncated or corrupted).
- ~/.ssh/etl_warehouse.pub unchanged.
- ~/.ssh/config unchanged.
- /home/user/etl/sync_to_warehouse.sh unchanged.
- /home/user/etl/staging/ files unchanged.

Anti-shortcut guards:
- The fix must be the chmod of the private key, not deleting the key, not modifying the script to use a different key, not disabling SSH's permission check (which can't be done anyway).
- `stat -c %a ~/.ssh/etl_warehouse` must return 600 or 400, not any other value.
name: task_000000_718511fb
